segments.s514-g73: دگر here is an adverb meaning “then/again,” placed before گفت (the past tense 'he said') to mark a further remark. چون introduces a temporal/subordinate clause (“when/since”) and takes the verb بازگشت (here a verb) at the end; لشکرت is the possessive form “your army/host.” The whole segment = “then he said, when your army returned/returns.”
segments.s514-g74: تو is the emphatic/referential pronoun “you.” تنها modifies the predicate and means “alone.” نمانی is the present/subjunctive form of ماندن with the negative prefix ن- omitted in translation here as part of the clause’s sense (“you will not remain”); grammatically it is a second/third‑person verbal form expressing the expected outcome. بر + ین (here the demonstrative 'this' written as ین) form the prepositional phrase برین ‘on this’; پهن دشت is the adjective + noun “wide plain.”
segments.s514-g75: همانا is an emphatic particle meaning “truly/behold.” پس is a connective adverb here meaning “then/therefore.” هرکسی بنگری is a generic conditional/proverbial clause: هرکسی ‘whoever’ + بنگری, the present/subjunctive form of نگر(یدن) used to mean “looks at/considers” in a general sense.
segments.s514-g76: فراوان is an adverb/adjective “abundant/much” modifying غم ‘sorrow.’ غم زندگانی is a genitive phrase 'the sorrows of life' (زندگانی qualifying غم). خوری is a verbal form of خوردن used idiomatically to mean 'to experience/suffer' (literally 'to eat' but here 'to befall/undergo'); it presents the consequence for the generic subject of the previous clause.
segments.s514-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (not 'as' or 'if'); آمد is the simple past 'came'; سکندر is the name 'Alexander'. The prepositional phrase به اسکندری marks destination/location ('to/into Alexandria') and follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s514-g2: جهان را uses را as the definite object/topic marker so 'the world' is topicalized; دگرگونه is an adjective 'different'; شد is the past of شدن 'became' (copular/inchoative); داوری is the predicate noun 'judgment' or 'order'. Word order is poetic: 'the world's judgment became different.'
segments.s514-g3: به هامون is a locative/directional phrase 'into/to the plain' (هامون = plain/expanse). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن 'they placed/put'; صندوق اوی = 'his chest/box' (اوی here marks possession: 'his'). The whole clause means 'they set his chest in the plain.'
segments.s514-g4: زمین شد is 'the earth became' (زمین subject + شد 'became'); سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely'; پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'); گفت‌وگوی is the genitive 'of conversation/talk'. Together: 'the earth became throughout full of conversation.'
segments.s514-g5: به اسکندری is a locative 'in/at Alexandria'; the list کودک و مرد و زن enumerates people of all ages/sexes ('children, men, and women') and functions as the participants/topic of the clause. The preposition + list sets the scene/place.
segments.s514-g6: به تابوت او is 'to/at his coffin' (possession indicated by او); بر شدند is a compound use where بر + شدن gives the sense 'rose/moved up/approached' (they ascended or gathered around); انجمن is the subject 'assembly/meeting' — word order is inverted poetically: 'an assembly went up to his coffin.'
segments.s514-g7: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; برگرفتی is 2nd-person past of برگرفتن used here in a hypothetical sense 'if you were to take' (i.e., take a census); ز مردم شمار literally 'the counting from the people' = 'a count of the people'. The protasis uses past form for a counterfactual/potential action, a common poetic usage.
segments.s514-g8: مهندس is a vocative addressing the person responsible for measuring/counting (here 'surveyor' or 'engineer'); فزون آمدی is a verbal phrase meaning 'would be found extra/in increase' (فزون 'in excess/more' + آمدی 2nd-person past used modally); صد هزار is the amount 'a hundred thousand'. The clause reports the expected surplus if the count were taken.
segments.s514-g49: بریدی is a past tense verb addressing 'you' (you left/withdrew), followed by the coordinating conjunction و. زر داری اندر کنار is a present-tense possession clause: زر (gold) + داری (you have) with the locative phrase اندر کنار 'at your side/alongside'. Word order is Subject‑verb (implicit subject 'you') then 'and' then direct object + possession verb + locative.
segments.s514-g50: به رسم کیان is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'by/according to the custom of the kings' (به + nounِ رسم + genitive/attributive کیان). زر و دیبا مدار is an admonition where مدار functions idiomatically to mean 'do not regard/measure/let be governed by' (classical/poetic negative admonitive). The noun phrases زر و دیبا (gold and brocade) are the objects being qualified by مدار.
segments.s514-g51: دگر گفت here means 'another said' (دگر = another/other + گفت 'said'), and پرسنده پرسد کنون is a verbless/clipped report clause: پرسنده (the questioner) + پرسد (he asks) + کنون (now). Classical narrative often drops a full 'he said' before reported speech; word order is topic (pَرسنده) + finite verb (پرسد).
segments.s514-g52: چه یاد آیدت is an interrogative phrase: چه 'what' + یاد 'remembrance/trace' + آیدت 'comes to you' (present/subjunctive/impersonal form with enclitic -ت for 'to you'). پاسخ رهنمون is a nominal phrase 'a guiding answer' or 'answer of guidance' (پاسخ 'answer' + رهنمون 'guide/guidance') that completes the question. The clause reads 'what remembrance/answer of guidance comes to you?'.
segments.s514-g53: که at the start links this clause to the preceding speech and introduces the content of the question; here the clause is an accusatory question: خون بزرگان 'the blood of the great ones' (object) + چرا ریختی 'why did you shed/pour' (چرا 'why' before the past-tense verb ریختی 'you poured/shed'). The verb ریختی is past 2nd person singular.
segments.s514-g54: به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with difficulty/ardently/harshly' formed by به + سختی; then به گنج اندر آویختی is a second coordinated clause: به گنج 'to/for the treasure' (prepositional) + اندر (in/among) + آویختی, past tense 'you clung/hung on'. The verb آویختی is used figuratively 'to cling to/attach oneself to' (2nd person past).
segments.s514-g55: خنک is a short exclamatory/predicative adjective applied to the subject phrase that follows: آنکسی 'that one/whoever' + کز بزرگان 'who is from/among the great ones' + بمرد. بمرد here is an archaic/subjunctive/past form meaning 'died' or 'who died' in relative use. The whole segment amounts to a felicitation: 'fortunate is he who died among the great.'
segments.s514-g56: ز گیتی جز از نیک‌نامی نبرد is a negative summarizing clause: ز گیتی 'from the world' + جز 'except' + از نیک‌نامی 'of good name' + نبرد. Here نبرد functions as a past negative verbal idea 'he did not take' (classical usage: نبَرد = 'he did not carry away'), so the sense is 'he took from the world nothing except a good name.' The word order is prepositional phrase + exception marker + object phrase + verb.
segments.s514-g41: کنون‌ای is a vocative temporal phrase: کنون 'now' followed by the vocative particle ای (here attached), addressing someone. هنرمند modifies مرد and functions as an epithet ('skillful'); مرد دلیر is a nominal phrase 'brave man/hero' (مرد is the head noun, دلیر an adjective). Word order is emphatic: the address comes first (Now, O skillful brave man).
segments.s514-g42: ترا here is represented orthographically as the clitic ت (second-person object) plus the object marker را; together they mark 'to/for you' as a definite object. زر زرد places the noun 'gold' before its adjective 'yellow' (a common poetic order). آوریدست is an archaic/periphrastic perfect meaning 'has brought' (equivalent to modern آورده است). زیر is a locative/prepositional adverb 'beneath/under' and attaches to the clause to indicate position or relation.
segments.s514-g43: دگرگفت is a single lexical unit here meaning 'another said' / 'he replied' (دگر- 'another' + گفت 'said'). دیبا بپوشیده‌ای is a predicate describing the addressee: دیبا 'brocade' + بپوشیده‌ای 'you are clad/you have been clothed' (ب‑ prefix + پوشیده + ـی second-person ending) — a perfect/resultative or stative form addressing someone as 'clad in brocade.'
segments.s514-g44: نپوشیده here functions as an adjective/participle 'uncovered/unveiled' (negative participle of پوشیدن) used substantively; را is the definite-object marker that places focus/emphasis on that noun. نیز 'also' links the next clause, رخ 'face' is the direct object, and دیده‌ای is the perfect/past participial form with second-person ending 'you have seen' (دیده + ـای), so the clause reads literally 'have you also seen the face of the unveiled?'. Note the object (را-marked noun) precedes the verb, as typical.
segments.s514-g45: کنون is an adverb 'now' that begins the command. سر ز دیبا برآور is an imperative clause: سر 'head' is the object, ز 'from' marks the source (from the brocade), and برآور is the imperative 'lift/raise' (archaic form corresponding to برآور/برآورَد). که introduces the following subordinate clause giving the reason or content ('for/that').
segments.s514-g46: همی is a classical verbal prefix indicating continuous or emphatic aspect ('still/ever'); جویدت is an archaic present/persistent form 'seeks you' (جوید + ـت object‑clitic), where the object clitic -ت refers to 'you'. یاره is a vocative or nominative addressing word 'O beloved/companion'. و links the two items تخت عاج is a compound noun 'ivory throne' (تخت 'throne' + عاج 'ivory'). Overall: 'for the crown still seeks you, O beloved, and (so does) the ivory throne.'
segments.s514-g47: دگر (another) precedes گفت ('said'), forming 'another said' (note: the provided senses for گفت do not list the verb sense; here it is the past-tense verb 'said'). کز is the archaic contracted form of 'که+از' or a relative conjunction meaning 'that/from which' introducing the content clause. ماه‌رخ is an adjective used substantively 'moon-faced' (i.e., beautifully-faced person), and بندگان is the noun 'servants/subjects/worshippers' serving as the subject of the following description.
segments.s514-g48: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (equivalent to از). چینی و رومی are adjectives/national descriptors 'Chinese and Rûmi/Byzantine' modifying the head noun پرستندگان, which means 'worshippers/devotees'. The phrase thus gives the origin/identity of those devotees: 'worshippers from China and Rûm.' Word order places the preposition + modifiers before the noun as a unit.
segments.s514-g65: ببینی is the classical 2nd-person present/subjunctive (prefix ب- + verb) meaning 'you behold/see' and here functions as an exhortation or present-tense narrative 'you see'. کنون is the adverb 'now' placed after the verb in poetic inversion. بارگاه بزرگ is a noun + adjective phrase 'great palace/royal court' with the adjective following the noun, the normal order in Persian.
segments.s514-g66: This is a participial/relative construction: جهانی ('a world, worldly') modified by جدا کرده, a perfective participle meaning 'having separated' or 'that has separated' (کرده functioning as resultative/perfect). از introduces the source 'from the sheep', and گرگ at the end is the agent 'the wolf' responsible for the action — classical Persian often places the agent after the participle without an overt connective. Overall: 'a world which the wolf has separated from the sheep.'
segments.s514-g67: دگر گفت = 'he said again' (دگر here = 'again'). کاندر is a fused classical locative/conjunction meaning 'that in' or 'wherein / in that', introducing the location clause. سرای سپنج is a genitival/nominal phrase 'the house/household of Sepanj' (سپنج treated as a name or specific place); in poetry proper names and places often follow سرای.
segments.s514-g68: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. داشتی is the past/imperfect form of داشتن 'you held/kept' (here past 2nd person: 'did you keep/subject yourself'). خویشتن را is the reflexive object 'yourself' with را as the object marker. به رنج means 'into/under hardship' — به + noun expresses being in a state or condition.
segments.s514-g69: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('that / because'), بهر تو = 'for you' or 'on your account' (بهر = 'for, to'), این آمد = 'this came/happened', and از رنج تو = 'from your suffering' — literal order is 'that for you this came from your suffering', i.e. 'because of your suffering this happened to you.'
segments.s514-g70: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' or 'a single'. تنگ تابوت شد is a verbless nominal predication with شد ('became') as the verb: 'became a narrow coffin' (تنگ = 'narrow', تابوت = 'coffin'). گنج تو is the possessor phrase 'your treasure' (noun + possessor). The clause reads: 'A single narrow coffin became your treasure.'
segments.s514-g71: نجویی is from the root جست‌/جوی 'seek' used here as a 2nd-person imperfect/present-future form ('you will seek' / 'you seek'); in classical Persian forms the exact tense/mood can be fluid, but here it conveys an action directed at the addressee. همی is the iterative/progressive particle 'continually' or 'still'. ناله بوق را is a noun phrase (ناله 'lament/cry' governed by the object-marking را) meaning 'the lament/cry of the horn/trumpet.'
segments.s514-g72: به + سند here gives a direction or destination 'to Sind' (سند can be the region Sind) or possibly 'to the record/document' depending on sense; آمدت is the 3rd-person-past-verb + 2nd-person enclitic meaning 'it came to you' or 'you received'. بند صندوق را is the object: بند = 'fetter/fastening/lock' and صندوق = 'chest/box', with را marking that object. So the clause says roughly 'to Sind came to you the fastening/lock of the chest' (i.e. you received the sealed/fastened chest).
segments.s514-g57: دگر گفت is an adverb + verb phrase: دگر ('again/another') modifies گفت ('said'); here it functions as 'he added' or 'he again said'. روز تو اندرگذشت is a nominal time phrase with possessive تو ('your') and the poetic past اندرگذشت ('passed/has passed'); اندرگذشت is an intransitive past used here in the sense 'has passed' (not 'died'). Word order is adverb–verb followed by the clause whose predicate is the past verb اندرگذشت.
segments.s514-g58: زبانت is the noun زبان with the 2nd-person possessive clitic -ت ('your tongue/speech'). ز (poetic) marks cause or source ('from / because of') and takes گفتار ('speech') as its object. بیکار گشت is a compound predicate: بیکار ('idle/inactive') + past گشت ('became'), together 'became idle' or 'fell silent/was rendered useless for speaking.' The prepositional phrase ز گفتار ('from speech') gives the reason or domain.
segments.s514-g59: هرانکس که ... دید is a fused relative: هرانکس ('whoever') + که ('who/that') introduces the subordinate clause; او is the resumptive pronoun 'he' (classical style) functioning as the subject. تاج و تخت تو is a coordinated possessive noun phrase ('your crown and throne'), and دید is the past of 'see/behold'. The order is relative pronoun + (resumptive) pronoun + object phrase + past verb.
segments.s514-g60: عنان ('reins, bridle') is the direct object of the implied action; از بزرگی ('because of/of greatness') gives the motive. بباید is an archaic/classical modal form ('must/ought to') and takes the following verb as its complement. کشید is the verb 'to draw/pull' (here idiomatically 'draw the reins in'), but the provided sense list does not include this exact meaning, so the matching sense is not available in the supplied options.
segments.s514-g61: که introduces a clause that explains or substantiates the previous statement. بر کس نماند is literally 'did not remain on/for anyone' — نماند is past negative (ن‑ prefix + ماندن) meaning 'did not remain/ endure.' چو here means 'as' or 'just as' and introduces the comparison 'as it did not remain for you' (بر تو نماند). The repeated structure is a classical rhetorical parallelism.
segments.s514-g62: درخت بزرگی is the noun phrase 'a great tree' (درخت + genitive modifier بزرگی = 'tree of greatness' / 'great tree'). چه is an interrogative/exclamatory particle ('what/how') introducing a rhetorical question. باید is the modal 'must/ought' and is followed by the classical negative-subjunctive نشاید (ن‑ prefix + شایِدن) meaning 'it is not fitting/it cannot be' — together the clause poses the rhetorical question 'what can a great tree do?' or 'what could possibly remain?'.
segments.s514-g63: دگر گفت again = 'he said again'. کردار تو is the possessed noun phrase 'your conduct/deed'. بادگشت is a compound noun (باد 'wind' + گشت 'turn') used metaphorically to mean 'change' or 'reversal' — here it is the predicate: 'your conduct underwent a reversal.' Word order places the predicate noun after the subject phrase in a classical poetic style.
segments.s514-g64: سر سرکشان is a genitival construction: سر ('head' / 'chief') + سرکشان (plural/agent form meaning 'the rebellious ones' or 'the insurgents'); together 'the heads/chiefs of the rebels.' از تو آزاد گشت is a predicate: از تو ('from you' — indicating separation or freedom from someone's power) + آزاد ('free') + گشت ('became') = 'became free from you.' Note that the supplied senses for سرکشان do not match the intended lexical meaning, so that entry is not available among the given senses.
segments.s514-g33: دگر گفت is a formula meaning “he said again” (دگر = again). چون introduces a temporal clause here and takes the verb in the subordinate clause; بیندت is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive with the enclitic object ت “you” (literally “he sees you” or “when he sees you”), and اوستاد is the noun “the master/teacher,” the subject of بیندت.
segments.s514-g34: بیاموزد is used in the sense “(he) will teach” (agentive transitive), followed by the object phrase آن چیز (literally “that thing”). کت here is the rare sense “book,” and the clause ends with نیست یاد — “is not (to be) learned/remembered from” (یاد functioning as ‘learning/knowledge’/‘remembrance’ in this context). Word order is archaic: verb — object — negative copula — noun indicating manner/source.
segments.s514-g35: دگر گفت = “he said again.” کز introduces a subordinate clause here (≈ “since/for/that”), مرگ = “death,” and چون تو نرست uses چون = “as/since” + تو (you) with نرست, here best read in the sense “was not saved/escaped” (did not survive/escape). Together the clause gives the reason — “since/from the fact that you did not escape death.”
segments.s514-g36: به + بیشی سزد‌گر is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/for a more‑fitting one” (بیشی = “more,” سزدگر = “one who is fitting/appropriate”). نیازیم is 1st‑person plural present “we need/require” (here “we must call on”), and دست at the end means “hand” in the sense of “help/aid.” The whole line: “we need the hand of a more fitting one.”
segments.s514-g37: دگر گفت = “he said again.” کای is the vocative “O!” addressing someone; برتر از ماه و مهر is a comparative phrase “superior/loftier than the moon and (the) sun” (ماه = moon; مهر in classical poetry functions as the sun or its radiance). Word order places the epithet after the vocative for emphasis.
segments.s514-g38: چه = “why/what” (here serving as “why”), پوشی is 2nd‑person singular present “you cover/veil” (پوشی from پوشیدن), and همی indicates continued or habitual action (“do you always/now”). ز انجمن means “from/out of the assembly/gathering,” and خوب‌چهر modifies the implied object: “your fair face.” Literal order is rhetorical: “Why do you cover (پوشی) (your) fair face from the assembly?”
segments.s514-g39: دگر گفت = “he said again.” مرد فراوان هنر is a noun phrase where مرد = “man/hero” is qualified postpositively by فراوان هنر = “abundant in art/skill” (a common poetic order: noun + its attributes).
segments.s514-g40: بکوشد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive “(he) strives/attempts.” که introduces the purpose/complement (“that/so that”), and چهره بپوشد is a subordinate verb phrase “(he) may cover his face” (بپوشد = to cover/conceal). به زر literally “with gold” marks the means or material. Overall: “he strives so that he may cover his face with gold.”
segments.s514-g25: دگر گفت is a repeated formula 'another said' (دگر = 'another'); کسودی is a noun meaning a kind of wasting/melancholy and is the grammatical subject complemented by the following prepositional phrase از درد و رنج, where از introduces the cause/reason ('from/because of'). Word order is subject–predicate with the cause phrase following the subject.
segments.s514-g26: هم begins the continuation and links this clause to the previous idea ('also'); از again introduces the source/cause ('from' / 'because of'); جستن is the infinitive 'the seeking / pursuit' and functions as a verbal noun (masdar) governed by از; پادشاهی and گنج are the objects of that pursuit, coordinated by و.
segments.s514-g27: چون پیش داور شوی is a temporal/conditional clause introduced by چون 'when/as'; پیش داور 'in the presence of the judge' places the action; شوی is the second‑person singular subjunctive/present form of شدن used here with a sense close to 'you are/you come to be' (i.e., 'when you stand/appear'). The clause sets the time/condition for the following imperative/instruction.
segments.s514-g28: This clause uses a parallel/anaphoric structure: همان ... همان ('the same ... the same') to command that one should 'do the same.' بر here functions toward/against or 'do unto' (marking the object of the action); که introduces a relative/comparative idea 'that/which'; کشتی is the thing referred to (here 'ship' in its literal sense); بدروی (an older/poetic form related to درود/درود فرستادن) carries the sense 'greet'/'show homage' — together: 'do to him what you did to the ship; greet him likewise.'
segments.s514-g29: بی‌دستگاه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'without device/artifice' or 'plainly/unassisted'; آن بود is a simple past copular clause ('that/he was'). The sequence presents a characterizing clause: 'He was, without guile, ...'.
segments.s514-g30: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; ریزنده is an agent‑noun formed from ریختن, 'the one who causes to flow / the shedder'; خون functions as the direct object 'blood' (here 'bloodshed'); شاهان is the plural 'kings'; بود is the past copula repeating the assertion ('was'). So: 'who was the shedder of kings' blood.'
segments.s514-g31: ما چون تو باشیم زود is an optative/conditonal expression: ما چون تو باشیم literally 'if/when we were like you' with باشیم functioning as present‑subjunctive/optative ('would be' / 'could be'); زود ('soon/quickly') modifies the whole clause emphasizing how quickly that change would happen. The phrase expresses wish or astonishment ('how soon we would be like you').
segments.s514-g32: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/since'; بودی is the second‑person past ('you were'); تو is the pronoun 'you'; چون here means 'like' and links تو to the simile گوهر 'a gem'; نابسود is a descriptive adjective applied to گوهر — in the line it functions as a negative compound meaning not worn out/unfading in older/poetic usage (the literal glosses given are more like 'fruitless / not yielding', so the exact poetic nuance is archaising).
segments.s514-g17: حکیمان (plural) is the subject ‘the sages’; رومی is an adjectival qualifier ‘Roman/Byzantine’ placed after the noun as is common in poetry; شدند is the past plural of شدن ‘became/assembled’ and takes انجمن (assembly) as its predicate complement: literally ‘the Roman sages became (an) assembly’ — i.e. they gathered into an assembly.
segments.s514-g18: یکی گفت is the common formula ‘one (someone) said’; کای is an archaic vocative particle ‘O’; پیل is the noun ‘elephant’ and the following رویینه تن is a descriptive phrase ‘armored-bodied’ (رویینه ‘armored/iron-like’, تن ‘body’) — together forming the address ‘O elephant of armored body’. Note گفت functions as the speech verb introducing direct speech.
segments.s514-g19: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from / since’ giving a causal sense; پایت is ‘your foot’ (noun + possessive suffix); که introduces a relative clause ‘that/which’; افگند is a past form meaning ‘fell/was cast (down)’; و is the conjunction ‘and’; جانت is ‘your soul/ life’ (with possessive suffix); the second که again introduces a relative clause; خست is an adjectival/predicative past form meaning ‘is/has become weary’ — together: ‘since your foot has fallen and your life is weary’.
segments.s514-g20: کجا is the interrogative ‘where’ (here: ‘where are…’); آن همه is a demonstrative + quantifier ‘all that / so much’; حزم is an abstract noun ‘prudence, carefulness’; و رای is ‘and counsel/advice’ (رای here = plan/counsel); و نشست is the final coordinate — نشست here names a settledness/being-seated/composure (not a literal meeting) giving the list ‘prudence, counsel, and composure’.
segments.s514-g21: دگر گفت ‘another said’ (دگر = another); چندین is a quantifier ‘many / several’; نهفتی is a second-person past form ‘you hid/concealed’ (the -ی ending marks 2nd person past in classical style); تو is the explicit pronoun ‘you’; زر is the object ‘gold/treasure’ — poetic word order places the object near the verb.
segments.s514-g22: کنون ‘now’ sets the time; زر is subject ‘gold’; دارد is present tense ‘has/holds’; تنت را is the object ‘your body’ with the definite/object marker را; به بر is a small adverbial phrase meaning ‘to its bosom / on its breast’ (به+بر = upon/against the bosom). Grammatically: ‘now gold holds your body to its breast.’
segments.s514-g23: دگر گفت ‘another said’; کز is the fused preposition/conjunction ‘که + ز’ here meaning ‘from’/‘because of’ (hence ‘from your hand’); دست تو is ‘your hand’ (hand = power/patronage); کس in this negative context means ‘no one’; نرست is past-form ‘grew/prospered’ — together ‘from your hand no one prospered’ (i.e. nobody flourished under you).
segments.s514-g24: چرا is the interrogative ‘why’/‘what use’; سودی‑ای is an exclamatory/elliptical phrase ‘what profit / what gain’ (سودی = profit; the ending -ای is vocative/exclamatory here); شاه is the vocative ‘O king’; با is the preposition ‘with/by’; مرگ دست literally ‘death’s hand’ (مرگ = death, دست = hand/power) — together: ‘O king, what profit is there with the hand of death?’ (i.e. what good does it do to seek power that ends in death).
segments.s514-g9: This is a nominal/prepositional chunk: حکیم ارسطالیس (the epithet + proper name) identifies the agent. پیش اندرون is a terse, poetic locution meaning ‘before / at the entrance of the inner place’ or ‘in the presence of the inner chamber’ (پیش used as a prepositional word + اندرون ‘inner quarters’). حکیم here is the honorific ‘sage’ rather than ‘physician’.
segments.s514-g10: جهانی is used as a substantive/adjective ‘a worldly person’ (literally ‘worldly’). برو in this line functions poetically (read as a contracted/elliptical locution, effectively ‘standing before him / before him’) rather than the ordinary imperative ‘go’, so its ordinary sense does not fit exactly. دیدگان پر ز خون is a tight noun phrase ‘eyes full of blood’ where پر ز = ‘full of’ (ز = the poetic preposition ‘of/from’).
segments.s514-g11: برآن Tنگ صندوق is a compact phrase meaning ‘upon that narrow chest/coffer’. بنهاد is the past form of نهادن ‘to put/lay’ and here means ‘placed/laid’; it governs دست ‘hand’ (بنهاد دست = ‘laid (his) hand (upon it)’). The word order (object/locative before verb) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s514-g12: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase ‘thus he said’ (چنین = thus). گفت here is the past tense ‘said’ (the provided sense list gives nouns like ‘conversation’, so the precise verbal sense is not listed). کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ addressing the addressee; شاه یزدان‌پرست is the epithet ‘king, God-worshipper’ (an honorific address beginning his speech).
segments.s514-g13: کجا begins the interrogative ‘where’. آن هش و دانش و رای تو lists the three things being asked about: آن (that), هش (an archaic/poetic word for attention/wisdom — the provided senses don’t include this exact lexical meaning), دانش ‘learning/knowledge’, and رای ‘counsel/opinion’ followed by the possessive تو ‘your’. The three nouns are coordinated and address the addressee’s faculties.
segments.s514-g14: که introduces a relative/result clause ‘that...’. این تنگ تابوت شد جای تو = ‘this narrow coffin became your place’ — شد is the past of شدن ‘to become’. جای تو = ‘your place/position’ (i.e. your resting-place). Word order places the clause after the interrogative for rhetorical effect.
segments.s514-g15: به‌روز جوانی = ‘in the day/prime of youth’ (به‌روز here = ‘on the day / at the time’ in a poetic sense). برین is a contraction of بر این (‘upon/at this’), written here as بر + ین (the poetic/orthographic variant of این). مایه سال literally ‘the substance/prime of the year’ is a poetic image for the best season or prime. The phrase sets up the temporal/frame for the reproach.
segments.s514-g16: چرا is the interrogative ‘why’. خاک را is the object ‘the earth’ (را marks it as definite/direct object). برگزیدی is the past ‘you chose’ (گرزیدن/برگزیدن), and نهال is a vocative/metaphor ‘sapling’ (a young person). The order (object before verb) and the vocative at the end are typical of poetic address.

sentences.s514-s37: Then he said: when your army returns, you will not remain alone on this wide plain.
sentences.s514-s38: Truly, then, whoever looks (or considers) will suffer much sorrow in life.
sentences.s514-s1: When Alexander came to Alexandria, the world's judgment (order) became different.
sentences.s514-s2: They placed his chest in the plain; the earth became throughout full of talk.
sentences.s514-s3: In Alexandria, children, men, and women — an assembly went up to his coffin.
sentences.s514-s4: If you took a count of the people, O surveyor, you would find (it) a hundred thousand more.
sentences.s514-s25: You have withdrawn and have gold at your side; do not regard gold and brocade according to the kings' custom.
sentences.s514-s26: Another said: the questioner now asks — what recollection (or guiding answer) comes to you?
sentences.s514-s27: Why did you shed the blood of the great men, and why did you cling so stubbornly to treasure?
sentences.s514-s28: Blessed/fortunate is the one who died among the great; from the world he took nothing except a good name.
sentences.s514-s21: Now, O skillful, brave man, they have brought yellow gold to you beneath.
sentences.s514-s22: Another said, 'You who are clad in brocade — have you also seen the face of the unveiled?'
sentences.s514-s23: Now raise your head from the brocade, for the crown and the ivory throne still seek you, O beloved.
sentences.s514-s24: Another said that from the moon-faced (one) are worshippers — devotees from China and Rûm.
sentences.s514-s33: Now you behold a great royal court — a world that the wolf has torn away from the sheep.
sentences.s514-s34: He again said, 'Why did you keep yourself in that house of Sepanj in hardship?'
sentences.s514-s35: For because of your toil this befell you — your treasure became a single narrow coffin.
sentences.s514-s36: You will seek and continually lament the trumpet's cry; to Sind came to you the fastening of the chest.
sentences.s514-s29: He added, “Your day has passed, and your tongue has been made idle of speech.”
sentences.s514-s30: Whoever beheld your crown and throne must draw in their reins out of your greatness.
sentences.s514-s31: That which did not remain for you did not remain for anyone; what can the great tree do (then)?
sentences.s514-s32: He again said, “Your conduct has reversed; the heads of the rebels have become free from you.”
sentences.s514-s17: He said again, “When the master sees you he will teach you — that thing is not learned from a book.”
sentences.s514-s18: He said again, “Since you did not escape death, we must call on the hand of one more fitting.”
sentences.s514-s19: He said again, “O you loftier than moon and sun, why do you hide your fair face from the assembly?”
sentences.s514-s20: He said again, “A man rich in skill strives to cover his face with gold.”
sentences.s514-s13: Another said, "A wasting (or melancholy) born of pain and suffering, and also from the pursuit of kingship and treasure."
sentences.s514-s14: Another said, "When you stand before the judge, do to him the same as you did to the ship; greet him in the same way."
sentences.s514-s15: Another said, "He was, without artifice, the one who shed the blood of kings."
sentences.s514-s16: Another said, "How quickly we would be like you — for you were, like a jewel, unfaded/unwasted."
sentences.s514-s9: The Roman sages formed an assembly; one said, “O elephant of armored body…”
sentences.s514-s10: Since your foot has slipped and your life grown weary, where now is all that prudence, counsel, and composure?
sentences.s514-s11: Another said, “You have hidden many a treasure of gold; now gold holds your body to its bosom.”
sentences.s514-s12: Another said, “From your hand no one prospered; O king, what profit is there with the hand of Death?”
sentences.s514-s5: The sage Aristotle went up into the inner chamber and there saw a worldly man whose eyes were full of blood.
sentences.s514-s6: He laid his hand upon that narrow chest and thus spoke, 'O God-worshipping king,'
sentences.s514-s7: Where is that wit and learning and counsel of yours, that this narrow coffin became your place?
sentences.s514-s8: In the flush of your youth, at the prime of the year, why did you choose the earth for your portion, O sapling?
